metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact YAML content the config-as-artifact test must assert for the acli PATH/auth step — at minimum: the `sudo ln -sf "$HOME/.acli/acli" /usr/local/bin/acli` command string and the `acli jira auth login` invocation — so the test fails if either is changed or removed.
findings.finding: The config-as-artifact test described in AC1 is required to 'pin the acli PATH/auth step,' but neither the AC nor the What section specifies what YAML content the test must assert — the exact strings to check (e.g., `sudo ln -sf`, `acli jira auth login`, the symlink target `/usr/local/bin/acli`) are left to the executor's discretion. An executor could write a trivially passing assertion (e.g., `assert 'acli' in yaml_text`) that doesn't actually guard against drift in the auth step.
findings.location: AC1 / 'A config-as-artifact test pins the cron, the Jira env wiring, and the acli PATH/auth step'
findings.suggested_fix: In the AC1 text and/or the test module spec, add: 'The config-as-artifact test must assert (a) the string `sudo ln -sf` with target `/usr/local/bin/acli` appears in the workflow YAML, and (b) the string `acli jira auth login` appears in the workflow YAML — mirroring how the repo's existing YAML-artifact tests assert specific command strings, not just key presence.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the `_live_jira_ready` naming contract (the implicit coupling that triggers auto-marking) in CLAUDE.md or a contributing guide, so future Jira live test module authors know they must define this module-level symbol to be covered by the canary.
findings.finding: The `_live_jira_ready` naming convention is a novel implicit contract between the conftest auto-marking hook and every current/future Jira live test module: any new module that omits `_live_jira_ready` will silently fall outside the canary's coverage. This architectural decision is not captured in any CLAUDE.md, ADR, or design-doc update, making it discoverable only by reading the conftest.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief section to CLAUDE.md (or `tests/external/README.md` if one exists) under "Live Jira tests" stating: 'Every Jira-gated live test module MUST define a module-level `_live_jira_ready()` function; the conftest auto-marks all such modules with `jira_live` and the canary fails if all are skipped. A module that omits `_live_jira_ready` is invisible to the canary.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `timeout-minutes` (e.g. 15) at the job level (or per-step for the acli auth and pytest invocation) in `external-integration.yml` so a hung run fails fast rather than holding a runner for the 6-hour GitHub default.
findings.finding: The scheduled external job specifies no `timeout-minutes` for the job or the `acli` auth / pytest run steps, so a hung `acli` binary, a stalled Jira API call, or a blocking test can hold a GitHub Actions runner for up to the default 6-hour ceiling — silently consuming minutes and delaying any failure signal from the canary.
findings.suggested_fix: In the job definition: `timeout-minutes: 15`. For the pytest step, additionally pass `--timeout=120` (via `pytest-timeout`) so individual hung tests surface promptly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test (or extend the existing behavioral test) that exercises the `pytest_collection_modifyitems` hook directly: construct fake items with and without `_live_jira_ready` on their `.module`, run the hook, and assert that exactly the items with `_live_jira_ready` receive the `jira_live` marker — covering the hook's happy path AND the exclusion case.
findings.finding: The `pytest_collection_modifyitems` auto-marking hook — the self-maintaining mechanism that keys on `_live_jira_ready` to apply the `jira_live` marker — has zero test coverage. The behavioral test drives only `pytest_sessionfinish` with pre-constructed report objects that already carry the marker, completely bypassing the collection hook. A bug in the `_live_jira_ready`-keying logic (e.g., wrong attribute name, wrong predicate, import error in the hook) would cause zero items to receive the `jira_live` marker, the sessionfinish hook would see 0 collected and silently not fire, and the entire canary would be defeated — while all unit tests stay green.
findings.suggested_fix: In `tests/unit/test_external_canary.py`, add a test that creates mock `pytest.Item` objects (using `types.SimpleNamespace` for `.module`) where some modules define `_live_jira_ready` and some do not, calls the `pytest_collection_modifyitems` hook from `conftest.py` directly, and asserts that (a) items from modules with `_live_jira_ready` have the `jira_live` marker, and (b) items from modules without it do not.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm why the existing `live` marker (already in `pyproject.toml` with identical semantics: live Jira + acli + dedicated project) cannot be reused for the fail-on-all-skip hook instead of registering a new `jira_live` marker; if `jira_live` is truly distinct, document the distinction explicitly.
findings.finding: A `live` marker already exists in `pyproject.toml` with semantics identical to the proposed `jira_live` marker — both gate on live Jira credentials + acli + a dedicated test project. Introducing `jira_live` creates config-surface proliferation: two registered markers for the same concept, with no stated reason the existing `live` marker cannot be repurposed or renamed.
findings.location: pyproject.toml `[tool.pytest.ini_options].markers` / What section (marker registration) / AC line 2
findings.suggested_fix: Reuse the existing `live` marker: key the `pytest_collection_modifyitems` hook on `item.module` defining `_live_jira_ready` and apply `live` (not a new `jira_live`). The `pytest_sessionfinish` hook then counts `live`-marked items. This avoids adding a new config surface and keeps one canonical marker for live Jira tests.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the schedule trigger interacts with the `langfuse-trace` job — either scope the schedule to the `external` job only (e.g. via a `workflow_call` reuse or a job-level `if: github.event_name != 'schedule'` guard on `langfuse-trace`), or explicitly acknowledge and accept the weekly Langfuse stack invocation.
findings.finding: Adding `on.schedule` to external-integration.yml triggers BOTH the `external` job AND the `langfuse-trace` job weekly. The `langfuse-trace` job spins up a full ephemeral Langfuse Docker-compose stack, which is unintended, resource-expensive, and unrelated to the Jira canary purpose. The plan does not address this side-effect or specify that the schedule trigger should be conditional on job level.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `if: github.event_name != 'schedule'` to the `langfuse-trace` job so that the weekly scheduled run only executes the `external` job, while `workflow_dispatch` continues to run both.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the new `jira_live` marker with the existing `live` marker in pyproject.toml — either reuse `live` (renaming or repurposing) or explicitly document why a second marker is preferable and whether `live` should be removed.
findings.finding: A `live` marker is already registered in pyproject.toml [tool.pytest.ini_options].markers with a description ('live: live Jira reconciler-validation tests (story d01e); hit a dedicated test project + require JIRA_URL/JIRA_USER/JIRA_API_TOKEN + acli; excluded from the default run, self-skip without live env; GUARANTEE bilateral cleanup') that covers precisely the same population as the new `jira_live` marker the plan proposes. The plan does not address the existing `live` marker — whether `jira_live` replaces it, coexists with it, or is entirely separate.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `concurrency:` group to `external-integration.yml` (e.g. `group: external-integration / cancel-in-progress: false`) mirroring `reconcile-bridge.yml`, to prevent overlapping scheduled and manually-dispatched runs from colliding on the live Jira probe issues.
findings.finding: The plan adds `on.schedule` to `external-integration.yml` without adding a `concurrency:` group, leaving the scheduled run and a manually-dispatched `workflow_dispatch` run free to overlap. The live roundtrip test explicitly documents this hazard: 'concurrent runs collide on rate-limit backoff and orphan probe issues' (test_link_sync_roundtrip_live.py cleanup comment). The reference workflow (`reconcile-bridge.yml`) that the plan instructs to mirror verbatim DOES declare `concurrency: group: reconcile-bridge / cancel-in-progress: false` precisely to prevent this. The mechanism as specified will create real Jira artifacts that collide or orphan under overlap.
findings.suggested_fix:
Add to `external-integration.yml` immediately after the `on:` block:
```yaml
concurrency:
  group: external-integration
  cancel-in-progress: false
```
`cancel-in-progress: false` (not `true`) matches reconcile-bridge's choice: a running live-Jira pass should complete its cleanup `finally` block rather than being cancelled mid-flight and orphaning issues.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the `pytest_collection_modifyitems` hook spec, guard `item.module` access with `getattr(item, 'module', None)` (matching the robustness of the root conftest's `item.fspath` approach) to prevent `AttributeError` on non-`Function` item types.
findings.finding: The conftest `pytest_collection_modifyitems` hook accesses `item.module` to check `hasattr(item.module, '_live_jira_ready')`, but the plan does not verify that every `pytest.Item` collected under `tests/external/` exposes a `.module` attribute. The existing root conftest hook uses `item.fspath` (a universally available path attribute), not `item.module`. While `pytest.Function` items (standard test functions) always have `.module`, other item types (e.g., `DoctestItem` if pytest-doctest is enabled, or items from future test plugins) may raise `AttributeError`. The plan asserts this capability without acknowledging or guarding the difference.
findings.location: What / item 2 — conftest.py `pytest_collection_modifyitems` hook description; contrast with `tests/conftest.py` lines 81-88 which uses `item.fspath`
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `item.module defines _live_jira_ready` with `mod = getattr(item, 'module', None); if mod is not None and hasattr(mod, '_live_jira_ready'):` — exact same shape as the existing root conftest's try/except guard around `item.fspath`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit `permissions:` block (e.g. `permissions: read-all` or the minimal set actually needed) to the `external-integration.yml` workflow or its job, and mirror whatever `reconcile-bridge.yml` declares.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify GitHub Actions `permissions:` at the workflow or job level for `external-integration.yml`. GitHub's default GITHUB_TOKEN permissions can be broadly read/write (depending on repo settings), so the scheduled job may run with more privilege than needed — violating least-privilege for the Actions runtime itself.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `permissions: read-all` at the workflow level in `external-integration.yml` (or replicate the exact permissions block from `reconcile-bridge.yml` as the plan instructs for other conventions). If the job writes no artifacts, no elevated permissions are needed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a failure-notification step to `external-integration.yml` (e.g. a Slack/email notification step on `if: failure()`, or reference the repo's existing alerting convention) so that a failed scheduled canary run actively surfaces to the team.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no failure-notification mechanism for the scheduled canary run. A `pytest_sessionfinish` hook that exits nonzero only surfaces the failure in GitHub Actions' run status — which is silent unless someone actively monitors the Actions tab. The plan does not specify how operators are alerted when the Monday canary fires.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an `if: failure()` step at the end of the job that posts to the team's alerting channel (Slack webhook, GitHub issue creation, or email), or explicitly reference the repo's existing notification pattern from another workflow.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Tighten the `Download + verify + install acli` step in the new workflow so that a missing or blank `ACLI_SHA256` is a hard `exit 1` (not a `::warning::` that continues), ensuring binary integrity is always enforced; update the config-as-artifact test to assert the hard-fail guard is present.
findings.finding: The plan instructs the new workflow to reuse reconcile-bridge's acli download step 'verbatim in shape,' which makes sha256 integrity verification conditional (`if [ -n "${ACLI_SHA256:-}" ]`) — a missing or blank ACLI_SHA256 variable yields only a `::warning::` and continues, executing an unverified downloaded binary on the CI runner. The plan's AC mentions the step is 'pinned' but does not explicitly require the sha256 check to be mandatory (non-warning, hard-fail) in the new workflow.
findings.location: What § item 1 — 'Download + verify + install acli' step description; AC line: 'installs acli + symlinks it onto PATH + authenticates (the cache / download-verify-install / add-to-PATH-and-authenticate steps)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the conditional in the new workflow step with: `if [ -z "${ACLI_SHA256:-}" ]; then echo '::error::ACLI_SHA256 repo variable is unset. Set it to the sha256 digest of acli ${ACLI_VERSION}.'; exit 1; fi; echo "${ACLI_SHA256}  $HOME/.acli/acli.tar.gz" | sha256sum -c --strict`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the relationship between the new `jira_live` marker and the pre-existing `live` marker in pyproject.toml (line 433): state whether `jira_live` supersedes `live`, complements it, or targets a distinct population, and update the plan to handle any tests currently carrying `live` that lack `_live_jira_ready`.
findings.finding: The 'jira_live' marker is introduced without defining its relationship to the pre-existing 'live' marker in pyproject.toml (line 433), which has semantically identical scope: 'live Jira reconciler-validation tests...require JIRA_URL/JIRA_USER/JIRA_API_TOKEN + acli; GUARANTEE bilateral cleanup'. Two markers whose value vocabulary overlaps without a defined distinction is a schema/enum gap an implementer cannot resolve from the plan alone.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the sessionfinish hook decision rule to explicitly classify xfail/xpass outcomes: specify whether an xfailed `jira_live` test counts as 'executed' (suppresses the canary) or not, and cover this case in the behavioral test.
findings.finding: The sessionfinish hook's 'executed (passed or failed)' decision rule does not specify how xfail/xpass outcomes are classified. test_link_sync_live.py::test_link_primitives_roundtrip_live is @pytest.mark.xfail(strict=False) — if it xfails, pytest does NOT report it as 'passed' or 'failed'; it reports 'xfailed'. Under the plan's protocol, an xfailed jira_live test is neither 'executed' nor 'skipped', so the hook's executed-count logic is undefined for this outcome.
findings.location: ## What, item 2 (pytest_sessionfinish hook decision rule) / tests/external/test_link_sync_live.py line 71-78
findings.suggested_fix: Count 'xfailed' and 'xpassed' as 'executed' alongside 'passed' and 'failed', since they indicate the test ran against the live service (it just matched the expected failure mode). Update the hook spec to: 'FAILS iff ≥1 jira_live test was COLLECTED but none had outcome passed/failed/xfailed/xpassed'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the interaction between the new `pytest_sessionfinish` canary hook and the existing hook in `tests/conftest.py` (lines 369-390): document the ordering strategy (e.g. only escalate exitstatus, never lower it) and update the behavioral test to assert the hook correctly handles an already-nonzero exitstatus.
findings.finding: The plan places a second pytest_sessionfinish hook in tests/external/conftest.py that mutates session.exitstatus, while tests/conftest.py (lines 369-390) already contains a pytest_sessionfinish hook that sets session.exitstatus = pytest.ExitCode.TESTS_FAILED. Pytest calls all registered sessionfinish hooks in an unspecified order; if the repo-isolation hook runs after the new canary hook and overwrites a non-zero exitstatus with TESTS_FAILED (or vice versa), the final exit code is determined by hook ordering, not logic. The plan does not specify ordering or precedence.
findings.suggested_fix: In the new hook, use `session.exitstatus = max(session.exitstatus, EXIT_CODE)` or only set exitstatus when the current value is 0, so whichever hook fires last cannot lower an already-failing exit code.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a fallback or enforcement mechanism for the `_live_jira_ready` naming convention — for example: a collection-time warning when a `tests/external/*_live.py` module is collected without `_live_jira_ready`, a docstring in conftest.py stating the required convention, or a lint/test that enumerates external Jira modules and asserts each defines `_live_jira_ready`.
findings.finding: The self-maintaining guarantee ('a future Jira module is auto-covered') rests entirely on the implicit naming convention '_live_jira_ready'. The plan specifies no fallback or enforcement mechanism if a future module uses a different gate function name (e.g. '_jira_available', '_check_jira', or no gate function at all). A new module that omits '_live_jira_ready' silently escapes the canary with no warning.

The plan uses `pytest_terminal_summary / pytest_sessionfinish` interchangeably (with a `/` slash) to name the session hook that forces a nonzero exit, but only `pytest_sessionfinish` can actually mutate `session.exitstatus` to fail the run — `pytest_terminal_summary` receives a `terminalreporter` and `exitstatus` argument but has no mechanism to change the process exit code. An implementer who chooses `pytest_terminal_summary` will produce a hook that prints a warning but does NOT fail the session, breaking the entire canary mechanism silently.
findings.location: What §2: 'A session hook (pytest_terminal_summary / pytest_sessionfinish) that … FAILS the session (nonzero exit)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'A session hook (pytest_terminal_summary / pytest_sessionfinish)' with 'A `pytest_sessionfinish` hook that … sets `session.exitstatus = pytest.ExitCode.TESTS_FAILED`'. A companion `pytest_terminal_summary` hook can provide the human-readable report separately if desired.

findings.finding: The plan presents `pytest_terminal_summary` and `pytest_sessionfinish` as equivalent alternatives ('pytest_terminal_summary / pytest_sessionfinish') for failing the session with a nonzero exit code, but `pytest_terminal_summary` cannot set the process exit code — only `pytest_sessionfinish` + `session.exitstatus` can, as confirmed by the repo's own existing prior-art implementation (tests/conftest.py:369-390).
findings.location: What §2 'A session hook (pytest_terminal_summary / pytest_sessionfinish) that … FAILS the session (nonzero exit)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the slash notation with a single specified hook: 'A `pytest_sessionfinish` hook that, when REBAR_RUN_EXTERNAL is truthy, sets `session.exitstatus = pytest.ExitCode.TESTS_FAILED` iff ≥1 jira_live test was collected but zero executed — mirroring tests/conftest.py:390.'

findings.finding: The plan specifies the session hook as '`pytest_terminal_summary` / `pytest_sessionfinish`' (slash — never committing to one), then requires that it 'FAILS the session (nonzero exit)'. `pytest_terminal_summary` receives only the terminal reporter object and cannot directly mutate the process exit code; only `pytest_sessionfinish` exposes `session.exitstatus` for that mutation (as already demonstrated in tests/conftest.py line 390: `session.exitstatus = pytest.ExitCode.TESTS_FAILED`). This ambiguity leaves the exit-code mutation placement undefined — an implementor choosing `pytest_terminal_summary` will find the canary reports but does not actually fail CI.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '`pytest_terminal_summary` / `pytest_sessionfinish`' with '`pytest_sessionfinish`' throughout the plan. Use `pytest_terminal_summary` only for the human-readable report line (no exit-code side effect), and `pytest_sessionfinish` for the `session.exitstatus` mutation — matching the repo's existing pattern.

findings.finding: The 'What' section offers `pytest_terminal_summary` OR `pytest_sessionfinish` as the session hook, but `pytest_terminal_summary` cannot directly set the process exit code — only `pytest_sessionfinish` (via `session.exitstatus` mutation or raising SystemExit) can. The AC requires 'FAILS the session (nonzero exit),' so naming the wrong hook as an alternative risks an implementer choosing one that silently reports but does not fail.
findings.location: What §2 ('A session hook (`pytest_terminal_summary` / `pytest_sessionfinish`)…') vs AC item 2 ('FAILS the session (nonzero exit)')
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '`pytest_terminal_summary` / `pytest_sessionfinish`' with '`pytest_sessionfinish`' in What §2, and add a parenthetical: '(set `session.exitstatus = pytest.ExitCode.TESTS_FAILED` when the all-skip condition holds; `pytest_terminal_summary` may additionally be used for human-readable reporting but cannot set exit code).'

findings.finding: The plan specifies that the conftest session hook 'FAILS the session (nonzero exit)' when all jira_live tests skip, but leaves the implementation mechanism unspecified between `pytest_terminal_summary` and `pytest_sessionfinish`. `pytest_terminal_summary` is a reporting-only hook — calling `sys.exit()` from it raises SystemExit mid-report teardown, which can corrupt output and may be caught by pytest's own hook machinery, silently swallowing the failure. `pytest_sessionfinish` can correctly set `session.exitstatus` but runs before terminal summary. If the implementer chooses the wrong hook or wrong mechanism, the core canary logic silently does nothing — the session exits 0 even when all Jira tests skipped.
findings.location: What § 2 / AC item 2 — 'A session hook (pytest_terminal_summary / pytest_sessionfinish) that … FAILS the session (nonzero exit)'
findings.suggested_fix: Use `pytest_sessionfinish(session, exitstatus)` and set `session.exitstatus = pytest.ExitCode.TESTS_FAILED` (integer 1) when the condition is met. The behavioral test should assert `session.exitstatus` was mutated to the expected value after calling the hook.

findings.finding: The plan's fail-on-all-skip hook specifies it 'FAILS the session (nonzero exit)' via `pytest_terminal_summary / pytest_sessionfinish` (slash-ambiguous). `pytest_terminal_summary` is a reporting hook — it has no mechanism to force a nonzero process exit code. Only `pytest_sessionfinish` (by mutating `session.exitstatus`) or a separate `pytest_runtestprotocol` / `SystemExit` approach can actually alter the exit code. The behavioral test is specified to 'drive the hook function directly with constructed report objects', but if the hook is `pytest_terminal_summary`, driving it cannot validate that the session exit code is actually nonzero. This is a capability gap in the specified hook choice.
findings.suggested_fix: Use `pytest_sessionfinish(session, exitstatus)` and set `session.exitstatus = pytest.ExitCode.TESTS_FAILED` when the all-skip condition fires; the behavioral test should monkeypatch `os._exit` or use `pytest`'s `pytester` fixture to verify the actual process exit code.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
